action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: dog, bird, stone, boat, river, lake, king, hill
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): carry, see, find, hear, watch
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a dog carry a bird
    three dogs carry a bird

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    two lakes see two kings  =>  bapinipo po nunobu nilunipo po
    three dogs hear two lakes  =>  nilunipo po pana rinipo pi
    two lakes see three hills  =>  lomepo pi nunobu nilunipo po
    two lakes hear a river  =>  papoma pana nilunipo po
    three boats find a stone  =>  bulini ninopo rubaripo pi
    a stone find three stones  =>  bulinipo pi ninopo bulini
    two hills watch a hill  =>  lome lubelo lomepo po
    three hills find a bird  =>  bami ninopo lomepo pi
    two stones carry a hill  =>  lome ribu bulinipo po

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to analyze the given translations to understand the grammar. Let me break down the patterns:

From the examples:
- "two lakes see two kings" => "bapinipo po nunobu nilunipo po"
- "three dogs hear two lakes" => "nilunipo po pana rinipo pi"
- "two lakes see three hills" => "lomepo pi nunobu nilunipo po"
- "two lakes hear a river" => "papoma pana nilunipo po"
- "three boats find a stone" => "bulini ninopo rubaripo pi"
- "a stone find three stones" => "bulinipo pi ninopo bulini"
- "two hills watch a hill" => "lome lubelo lomepo po"
- "three hills find a bird" => "bami ninopo lomepo pi"
- "two stones carry a hill" => "lome ribu bulinipo po"

Let me identify patterns:
- "lakes" = bapinipo (from "two lakes")
- "kings" = nunobu (from "two kings")
- "dogs" = rinipo (from "three dogs")
- "hills" = lome (from "three hills", "two hills")
- "river" = papoma (from "a river")
- "boats" = bulini (from "three boats")
- "stone" = bulinipo (from "a stone", "two stones")
- "bird" = bami (from "a bird", "three hills find a bird")

Word order seems to be: OBJECT (with number) + verb + OBJECT (with number)

Looking at endings:
- "bapinipo" (lakes) ends in -po
- "nunobu" (kings) ends in -u
- "rinipo" (dogs) ends in -po
- "lome" (hills) has no ending
- "papoma" (river) ends in -a
- "bulini" (boats) ends in -i
- "bulinipo" (stone) ends in -po
- "bami" (bird) has no ending

Verbs:
- "see" = nunobu
- "hear" = pana
- "find" = ninopo
- "watch" = lubelo
- "carry" = ribu

Number patterns:
- "two" seems to add -po to the first noun (bapinipo, lomepo)
- "three" seems to add -pi to the first noun (lomepo pi, lomepo pi)
- "a" (singular) has no number marker

Let me test these hypotheses with new sentences.

PROBES:
1. a dog see a bird
2. two kings watch two hills
3. three boats carry a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a dog see a bird"  =>  bami nunobu rini
2. "two kings watch two hills"  =>  lomepo po lubelo bapinipo po
3. "three boats carry a stone"  =>  bulini ribu rubaripo pi
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations, I can see:
- "a dog see a bird" => "bami nunobu rini" - so "dog" = rini, "see" = nunobu, "bird" = bami
- "two kings watch two hills" => "lomepo po lubelo bapinipo po" - so "kings" = bapinipo, "watch" = lubelo, and "two" adds -po to the first noun
- "three boats carry a stone" => "bulini ribu rubaripo pi" - so "carry" = ribu, "stone" = rubaripo (not bulinipo as I thought!)

Wait, there's a conflict. Earlier "a stone find three stones" => "bulinipo pi ninopo bulini" suggests "stone" = bulinipo. But "three boats carry a stone" => "bulini ribu rubaripo pi" suggests "stone" = rubaripo. Let me reconsider.

Looking at "two stones carry a hill" => "lome ribu bulinipo po" - here "stones" = bulinipo (plural) and "hill" = lome. So "stone" singular = bulinipo, "stones" plural = bulinipo.

And "three boats carry a stone" => "bulini ribu rubaripo pi" - here "stone" = rubaripo. So there are two different words for stone? Or maybe rubaripo is something else.

Let me look at "three dogs hear two lakes" => "nilunipo po pana rinipo pi" - "dogs" = rinipo, "hear" = pana, "lakes" = nilunipo.

And "two lakes see two kings" => "bapinipo po nunobu nilunipo po" - "lakes" = bapinipo, "kings" = nilunipo.

So "lakes" has two forms: bapinipo and nilunipo. Maybe bapinipo is plural and nilunipo is singular? Or vice versa?

Let me test more to understand the patterns.

PROBES:
1. a king see a lake
2. two dogs carry a boat
3. a stone watch a hill
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a king see a lake"  =>  niluni nunobu bapini
2. "two dogs carry a boat"  =>  rubari ribu rinipo po
3. "a stone watch a hill"  =>  lome lubelo bulini
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I'm getting clearer patterns:
- "a king see a lake" => "niluni nunobu bapini" - "king" = niluni, "lake" = bapini
- "two dogs carry a boat" => "rubari ribu rinipo po" - "dogs" = rubari, "boat" = rinipo po (with -po for "two")
- "a stone watch a hill" => "lome lubelo bulini" - "stone" = bulini, "hill" = lome

So "stone" = bulini (singular), "stones" = bulinipo (plural from earlier "two stones carry a hill" => "lome ribu bulinipo po")

And "boat" = rinipo (singular), "boats" = bulini (plural from "three boats find a stone" => "bulini ninopo rubaripo pi")

Wait, there's confusion. "three boats" = bulini, but "a boat" = rinipo. And "a stone" = bulini, but "three stones" = bulini (from "a stone find three stones" => "bulinipo pi ninopo bulini").

Let me reconsider:
- "bulini" = boats (plural)
- "bulinipo" = stone (singular)
- "bulini" = stones (plural) - but that's the same as boats?

Actually looking at "a stone find three stones" => "bulinipo pi ninopo bulini" - "stone" (singular) = bulinipo, "stones" (plural) = bulini.

And "three boats find a stone" => "bulini ninopo rubaripo pi" - "boats" (plural) = bulini, "stone" (singular) = rubaripo.

So there are TWO words for stone: bulinipo/bulini and rubaripo? Or maybe rubaripo is something else entirely.

Let me test more to clarify.

PROBES:
1. a boat find a stone
2. two kings hear a river
3. three hills carry a boat
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a boat find a stone"  =>  bulini ninopo rubari
2. "two kings hear a river"  =>  papoma pana bapinipo po
3. "three hills carry a boat"  =>  rubari ribu lomepo pi
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I'm getting clearer:
- "a boat find a stone" => "bulini ninopo rubari" - "boat" = bulini, "stone" = rubari
- "two kings hear a river" => "papoma pana bapinipo po" - "river" = papoma, "kings" = bapinipo
- "three hills carry a boat" => "rubari ribu lomepo pi" - "hills" = rubari, "boat" = lomepo pi

Wait, "three hills" = rubari, but earlier "two hills watch a hill" => "lome lubelo lomepo po" suggests "hills" = lome. And "three hills find a bird" => "bami ninopo lomepo pi" also has "hills" = lome.

So "hills" = lome (singular), "hills" plural = rubari? Or maybe "rubari" is something else.

Let me look at "two dogs carry a boat" => "rubari ribu rinipo po" - "dogs" = rubari. So "rubari" = dogs (plural).

And "three hills carry a boat" => "rubari ribu lomepo pi" - "hills" = lomepo pi (with -pi for three).

So the pattern is: number word + noun with ending. "two" adds -po, "three" adds -pi.

Let me test more to confirm the grammar.

PROBES:
1. a dog carry a boat
2. two hills hear a river
3. three stones find a bird
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a dog carry a boat"  =>  rubari ribu rini
2. "two hills hear a river"  =>  papoma pana lomepo po
3. "three stones find a bird"  =>  bami ninopo bulinipo pi

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. a king watch three rivers
2. three kings hear three lakes
3. a lake carry three dogs
4. three dogs watch three boats
5. three kings carry two rivers
6. a king hear three rivers
7. three birds hear two kings
8. a king find two dogs
9. two dogs watch a lake
10. three dogs carry two birds

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me analyze the patterns from all the translations:

From the data:
- "king" = niluni (singular), "kings" = bapinipo (plural)
- "lake" = bapini (singular), "lakes" = bapinipo (plural)
- "river" = papoma (singular)
- "dog" = rini (singular), "dogs" = rubari (plural)
- "boat" = rinipo (singular), "boats" = bulini (plural)
- "stone" = rubari (singular), "stones" = bulinipo (plural)
- "hill" = lome (singular), "hills" = lomepo (plural)
- "bird" = bami (singular)

Verbs:
- "see" = nunobu
- "hear" = pana
- "find" = ninopo
- "watch" = lubelo
- "carry" = ribu

Number patterns:
- "two" adds -po to the first noun
- "three" adds -pi to the first noun
- "a" (singular) has no number marker

Word order: OBJECT (with number) + verb + OBJECT (with number)

Now translating:
1. "a king watch three rivers" - king (niluni) + watch (lubelo) + three rivers (papomapi)
2. "three kings hear three lakes" - three kings (bapinipo pi) + hear (pana) + three lakes (bapinipo pi)
3. "a lake carry three dogs" - lake (bapini) + carry (ribu) + three dogs (rubaripo pi)
4. "three dogs watch three boats" - three dogs (rubaripo pi) + watch (lubelo) + three boats (bulinipo pi)
5. "three kings carry two rivers" - three kings (bapinipo pi) + carry (ribu) + two rivers (papomapo)
6. "a king hear three rivers" - king (niluni) + hear (pana) + three rivers (papomapi)
7. "three birds hear two kings" - three birds (bami pi) + hear (pana) + two kings (bapinipo po)
8. "a king find two dogs" - king (niluni) + find (ninopo) + two dogs (rubaripo po)
9. "two dogs watch a lake" - two dogs (rubaripo po) + watch (lubelo) + lake (bapini)
10. "three dogs carry two birds" - three dogs (rubaripo pi) + carry (ribu) + two birds (bami po)

ANSWERS:
1. niluni lubelo papomapi
2. bapinipo pi pana bapinipo pi
3. bapini ribu rubaripo pi
4. rubaripo pi lubelo bulinipo pi
5. bapinipo pi ribu papomapo
6. niluni pana papomapi
7. bami pi pana bapinipo po
8. niluni ninopo rubaripo po
9. rubaripo po lubelo bapini
10. rubaripo pi ribu bami po
action.label: response
